channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: AI commerce revolution is coming. Are you ready ? #ecommerce #ai #seo #shopify #shopping #llm
executive_summary: The latest report from Silicon Valley spans 313 slides and highlights the major shift in AI development from focusing solely on model intelligence to considering system capabilities, costs, and distribution. By 2025 and 2026, AI builders will need to adapt to the rapidly changing economics of AI, particularly with models like GPT-5, Claude, and Gemini. This evolution will impact how AI is integrated into commerce, necessitating a strategic approach to infrastructure and model selection.
bullets: The capability-to-cost curve resets unit economics every few months, meaning that the cost-effectiveness of AI models is constantly changing. This is crucial for businesses to understand because it affects their budgeting and planning. It's like trying to buy a car where the price drops significantly every few months; if you wait, you might get a better deal.

Routing to the cheapest capable model can outperform default options, suggesting that businesses should be flexible in their AI model choices. This flexibility allows companies to save money while still accessing high-quality AI capabilities. Think of it like shopping for groceries: if you always buy the same brand, you might miss out on a cheaper, equally good alternative.

Answer engines will significantly impact distribution and AEO (Answer Engine Optimization) in web browsers. This means that how information is presented and retrieved online will change, affecting how businesses attract customers. Imagine a library where the librarian can instantly find the best book for you instead of you having to search through shelves; that’s how answer engines will work.

Power, permits, and water will become bottlenecks for scaling LLMs (Large Language Models). This is important because without sufficient resources, the growth of AI capabilities could be stunted. It's like trying to fill a swimming pool with a garden hose; if the water supply is limited, it will take a long time to fill it up.

AI builders must focus on multi-model routing, which allows them to switch between different AI models based on cost and capability. This strategy can enhance efficiency and reduce costs in AI operations. Think of it like a chef who uses different pots and pans for various dishes; using the right tool for the job makes cooking faster and easier.

The report emphasizes the need for clear-eyed planning regarding infrastructure limits, balancing automation benefits with execution risks. Companies that ignore these limits may face operational failures. It’s like building a bridge without considering how much weight it can hold; if you overload it, it could collapse.

The shift from model IQ to systems means that understanding the entire ecosystem of AI is more important than ever. This holistic view helps businesses strategize better and stay competitive. Imagine a sports team that not only trains its star player but also focuses on team dynamics; that’s how businesses should approach AI.

The implications for AI builders and investors include the necessity for strategic investments in infrastructure and model diversity. Those who fail to diversify may miss out on opportunities as the market evolves. Think of it like investing in a single stock; if that company fails, your entire investment is at risk.

By 2025 and 2026, AI builders will face new realities that require adaptability and foresight. This timeline is crucial for companies to prepare for upcoming changes in the AI landscape. It’s like preparing for a storm; if you wait until it arrives, it might be too late to secure your home.

The report indicates that the most read insights are essential for anyone involved in AI development or investment. Staying updated with these insights can provide a competitive edge. It’s like reading the latest news in sports; knowing the current stats can help you make better bets.

Open vs closed models will define the global AI landscape, affecting accessibility and innovation. Understanding these models is vital for businesses looking to leverage AI effectively. Think of it like choosing between an open-source software and a proprietary one; the former allows for more customization while the latter may offer more support.

The future of AI will emphasize individual agency and opportunities, suggesting that personal initiative will play a key role in AI development. This means that individuals can significantly influence the direction of AI technology. It’s like being an artist; your unique style can set you apart in a crowded gallery.
key_takeaways: Plan for the rapid changes in AI economics by reviewing your AI budget every few months. This is crucial because failing to adapt could lead to overspending on outdated models. If you don’t keep up, you might miss out on more cost-effective solutions.

Implement a strategy for multi-model routing immediately to enhance your AI operations. This flexibility can save costs and improve efficiency, which is vital in a competitive market. If you don’t do this, you risk falling behind competitors who are more agile.

Prepare for the infrastructure limits by assessing your current resources and planning for future needs. This proactive approach can prevent operational failures and ensure smooth scaling of AI solutions. Ignoring this could lead to significant setbacks when demand increases.

technical_terms.LLM: Large Language Model, a type of AI that can understand and generate human-like text. Think of it like a very advanced parrot that can mimic human speech and respond to questions intelligently.
technical_terms.AEO: Answer Engine Optimization, the process of optimizing how answers are retrieved and displayed in search engines. Like a librarian organizing books so that the most relevant ones are always easy to find.
technical_terms.multi-model routing: The ability to switch between different AI models based on their capabilities and costs. Think of it like a chef choosing the best cooking method for each dish to achieve the best flavor.
technical_terms.capability-to-cost curve: A graphical representation showing how the cost of AI models changes as their capabilities improve. Like a graph showing how the price of a car drops as it ages, even if it still runs well.
technical_terms.open models: AI systems that allow users to modify and improve the underlying code. Like an open-source software where anyone can contribute to making it better.
technical_terms.closed models: AI systems that restrict access to the underlying code and are controlled by a single entity. Think of it like a locked box where only the owner can access the contents inside.
technical_terms.infrastructure limits: The physical and technical constraints that affect the deployment and scaling of AI systems. Like the walls of a room that limit how much furniture you can fit inside.
technical_terms.answer engines: Systems designed to provide direct answers to user queries rather than just links to information. Think of it like a personal assistant who answers your questions without making you search for the information.
technical_terms.unit economics: The financial metrics that determine the profitability of a business model on a per-unit basis. Like calculating how much profit you make from each cupcake sold at a bakery.
technical_terms.distribution: The way in which products or services are delivered to customers. Think of it like a delivery service that ensures packages reach the right addresses efficiently.
